bare: - Reinhart, T. (1981). "Pragmatics and Linguistics: An Analysis of Sentence Topics." *Philosophica*, 27(1), 53-94. [Open Access](https://philpapers.org/archive/REIPAL.pdf)
claim_context: Hub uses Reinhart 1981 as the source for the 'What about X?' topic test for identifying sentence topics in information structure.
source_says: Reinhart's Philosophica 1981 paper argues that sentence topic is a pragmatic-linguistic phenomenon determined by both context and linguistic structure, and introduces the aboutness / 'what about X?' test for topic.
claim_support: Directly supports the hub's use of the 'What about X?' topic test; this paper is the canonical source for it.
impact_if_removed: Low — topic test is a canonical result; even without this citation the claim would stand, but Reinhart 1981 is the correct primary source.

bare: - Reiss et al. (1982). Diagnostic overshadowing. — [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/7102729/) [paywalled]
claim_context: Hub cites Reiss et al. 1982 as originator of the term 'diagnostic overshadowing' in the context of how autistic patients' complaints are attributed to their primary diagnosis.
source_says: PubMed 7102729 is Reiss, Levitan & Szyszko (1982) 'Emotional disturbance and mental retardation: diagnostic overshadowing,' AJMD 86(6):567-574, which coined and empirically validated the term.
claim_support: PubMed URL resolves to the correct paper and directly supports the hub's attribution of the term's origin.
impact_if_removed: Moderate — this is the foundational term origin; removing would weaken historical grounding but the concept is well established.

bare: - Reiss, S., Levitan, G. W., & Szyszko, J. (1982). "Emotional disturbance and mental retardation: diagnostic overshadowing." *American Journal of Mental Deficiency* 86(6): 567–574.
claim_context: Full-citation variant of the previous entry; same claim — origin of the term 'diagnostic overshadowing.'
source_says: Confirmed via PubMed and AAIDD review literature as the original 1982 paper by Reiss, Levitan & Szyszko.
claim_support: Full bibliographic record correctly attributes the term's origin; supports the claim exactly.
impact_if_removed: Low — duplicate of the PubMed-linked variant.

claim_context: Canonical Shapiro 1989 paper — foundational EMDR publication.
source_says: Canonical; F. Shapiro is the founder of EMDR and 1989 JTS 2(2):199-223 is the founding paper.

bare: - Shapiro, F. (2001). *Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing: Basic Principles, Protocols, and Procedures* (2nd ed.). Guilford Press.
claim_context: Canonical Shapiro 2001 EMDR clinical manual.
source_says: Canonical clinician reference.

key: shaw|2024|barriers to healthcare and a t
bare: Shaw, S. C. K. et al. (2024). "Barriers to healthcare and a 'triple empathy problem' may lead to adverse outcomes for autistic adults: A qualitative study." *Autism*, 28(7), 1746-1757.
claim_context: Hub cites Shaw et al. 2024 for the 'triple empathy problem' in autistic healthcare — breakdowns between patient, clinician, and system.
source_says: Shaw, S. C. K., Carravallah, L., Johnson, M., O'Sullivan, J., Chown, N., Neilson, S., & Doherty, M. (2024), Autism 28(7):1746-1757, DOI 10.1177/13623613231205629 — canonical triple-empathy-problem paper.
impact_if_removed: High — load-bearing for the triple-empathy-problem framing in healthcare contexts.

bare: Shaw et al. (2024) identify a **"triple empathy problem"** in healthcare...
claim_context: In-text reference to same Shaw et al. 2024 triple-empathy paper.
source_says: Same paper.

bare: - Shearing, V., et al. (2024). Allegiance effects in CPT for PTSD: A systematic review and meta-analysis. *Clinical Psychologist*. https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/13284207.2024.2347643.
claim_context: Hub cites Shearing et al. 2024 for a systematic review/meta-analysis of allegiance effects in CPT-for-PTSD research.
source_says: DOI 10.1080/13284207.2024.2347643 is a plausible Clinical Psychologist paper. Allegiance-effects-in-CPT is a known subfield; Shearing V. is an active PTSD researcher.
claim_support: Likely supports the claim; unable to fully confirm via fetch.
notes: DOI appears legitimate but direct verification blocked; recommend spot-check on final pass.

bare: - Sheley, E. (2024). "Criminalizing Coercive Control Within the Limits of Due Process." *Duke Law Journal.*
claim_context: Hub cites Sheley 2024 as legal-academic source on criminalization of coercive control.
source_says: Erin Sheley is an active law professor publishing on coercive control and criminal law. A 2024 Duke Law Journal piece on criminalizing coercive control is plausible. Unable to verify precise citation details.
claim_support: Topic and author consistent; exact venue/year unverified.
notes: Could not directly verify venue/year. Sheley's work on coercive control is real.

bare: - **Sheppard et al. (2016)** found that non-autistic participants had difficulty reading the mental states of autistic targets...
claim_context: Hub cites Sheppard et al. 2016 for the double-empathy finding that mental-state reading difficulty runs in both directions.
source_says: Sheppard, Pillai, Wong, Ropar & Mitchell (2016) 'How easy is it to read the minds of people with autism spectrum disorder?' JADD 46(4):1247-1254 — canonical double-empathy source.

key: sheppard|2016|how easy is it to read the min
bare: Sheppard, E., Pillai, D., Wong, G. T.-L., Ropar, D., & Mitchell, P. (2016). How easy is it to read the minds of people with autism spectrum disorder? *Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders*, 46(4), 1247-1254.
claim_context: Full canonical citation for Sheppard et al. 2016 double-empathy paper.
source_says: Canonical, DOI 10.1007/s10803-015-2662-8 verified.

bare: - **Sheppard et al. (2016)** showed that non-autistic observers had difficulty inferring emotion from autistic facial displays...
claim_context: In-text variant extending the Sheppard 2016 finding to facial displays specifically.
source_says: The Sheppard 2016 JADD paper is broader than facial displays — it uses vignette-based mental state attribution tasks, not face perception. The 'facial displays' framing may be a slight overstatement or conflation.
claim_support: Core claim (non-autistic observers have difficulty reading autistic mental states) is supported. The specific 'facial displays' extension may be beyond what the paper directly tests.
notes: SLIGHT OVERSTATEMENT: Sheppard 2016 is about mental state inference from short vignettes, not facial displays per se. The facial-display claim may need a different source (Brewer et al. 2016 or similar).
impact_if_removed: Low — duplicate with slight framing issue.

bare: **Sheppard, Pillai, Wong, Ropar, and Mitchell (2016)** showed that non-autistic participants are worse at reading autistic participants' mental states than they are at reading other non-autistic participants' mental states.
claim_context: In-text framing of the Sheppard 2016 finding.
source_says: Same canonical paper.

key: shield|2015|the use of sign language prono
bare: - [Shield, Meier & Tager-Flusberg (2015) The Use of Sign Language Pronouns by Native-Signing Children with Autism (PMC4474758)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4474758/)
claim_context: Hub cites Shield, Meier & Tager-Flusberg 2015 for evidence that native-signing autistic children reveal a specific perspective-transformation cost (with palm-orientation reversal as key evidence).
source_says: Verified: Shield, Meier & Tager-Flusberg (2015), JADD 45(7):2128-2145, DOI 10.1007/s10803-015-2377-x, PMC4474758. The paper's main finding is that signing children with ASD AVOID pronouns in favor of names, revealing that transparency of sign pronouns does NOT eliminate the difficulty — supporting a self/other-perspective hypothesis rather than an opacity hypothesis.
claim_support: The 2015 paper supports the broader 'specific perspective cost' framing but the palm-orientation-reversal evidence is actually from the follow-up fingerspelling paper (PMC9614108), not the 2015 paper. The hub conflates the two papers slightly, though it does cite the fingerspelling paper separately.
notes: NUANCE: 2015 paper's key finding is pronoun AVOIDANCE (not reversal). Palm-orientation reversal evidence is in the fingerspelling follow-up (PMC9614108). Hub should be precise about which finding is from which paper.
impact_if_removed: High — load-bearing for the 'specific cost, not global deficit' framing in deixis chapter.

bare: - **Shriberg et al. (2001)** analyzed speech and prosody in adolescents and adults with high-functioning autism and Asperger syndrome...
claim_context: Hub cites Shriberg et al. 2001 for the finding that autistic speech shows distributed prosodic differences (stress, phrasing, resonance), supporting the 'real, measurable differences' claim against pragmatic gaslighting.
source_says: Verified: Shriberg, Paul, McSweeny, Klin, Cohen & Volkmar (2001) 'Speech and prosody characteristics of adolescents and adults with high-functioning autism and Asperger syndrome,' J. Speech, Language, and Hearing Research 44(5):1097-1115, PMID 11708530. Found distributed differences in phrasing, stress, and resonance — matches hub claim exactly.
claim_support: Direct and accurate. Hub's characterization (distributed across the prosodic system, not a single articulatory feature) matches the paper's actual findings.
impact_if_removed: Moderate — supports the real-differences-are-real claim on pragmatic invalidation page.
